title: 5 Signs Your Pet Needs a Dental Cleaning
description: Bad breath isn't normal for pets. Learn the 5 warning signs that your dog or cat needs a professional dental cleaning from our Portland veterinary team.
author: Dr. Tracy Mento, DVM
content:
Most pet owners are surprised to learn that by age three, over 80% of dogs and 70% of cats have some degree of dental disease. At Forest Heights Veterinary Clinic, dental cleanings are one of our most common — and most impactful — procedures. Here are five signs it may be time to schedule one.

## 1. Bad Breath

It's a common misconception that dogs and cats are supposed to have bad breath. While their breath won't smell minty, a strong or foul odor is usually a sign of bacterial buildup, tartar, or even an infected tooth. If you notice your pet's breath getting worse over time, dental disease is the likely cause.

## 2. Yellow or Brown Buildup on Teeth

Lift your pet's lip and look at the teeth along the gum line. Healthy teeth should be white or off-white. If you see yellow, brown, or gray buildup — especially on the back molars and canines — that's tartar. Unlike plaque, tartar can't be removed with brushing and requires a professional cleaning under anesthesia.

## 3. Red, Swollen, or Bleeding Gums

Healthy gums should be pink and firm. Red, puffy, or bleeding gums (gingivitis) indicate inflammation caused by bacteria under the gum line. Left untreated, gingivitis progresses to periodontitis, which damages the bone and ligaments supporting the teeth and can lead to tooth loss.

## 4. Difficulty Eating or Dropping Food

If your pet seems interested in food but hesitates to chew, chews on one side, or drops kibble while eating, they may be experiencing oral pain. Cats in particular are masters at hiding discomfort — a change in eating habits may be the only sign you notice.

## 5. Pawing at the Face or Excessive Drooling

Pets with dental pain may paw at their mouth, rub their face against furniture, or drool more than usual. These behaviors can also indicate a broken tooth, an oral mass, or a foreign object stuck in the mouth — all of which need veterinary attention.

## What Happens During a Dental Cleaning?

Professional veterinary dental cleanings are performed under general anesthesia so we can thoroughly examine every tooth, take dental X-rays to evaluate the roots below the gum line, scale away tartar, and polish the teeth. If we find damaged or infected teeth, we'll discuss extractions with you before proceeding.

We use modern anesthesia protocols with continuous monitoring to keep your pet safe throughout the procedure. Most pets go home the same day and feel noticeably better within 24–48 hours.

If you've noticed any of these signs, call Forest Heights Veterinary Clinic at (503) 291-1757 to schedule a dental assessment. We provide pet dentistry services for dogs and cats in Forest Heights, Cedar Mill, Oak Hills, Bethany, and throughout NW Portland. Early treatment prevents pain and saves teeth.
